titleEn: Opinion | Why agricultural robots may never become much cheaper
titleZh: 农业机器人为何可能永远不会大幅降价？
summaryZh: 本文探讨了农业机器人价格不会像GPS、无人机等科技产品那样大幅下降的原因。核心观点是：机器人定价取决于其为农场创造的经济价值，而非制造成本。随着劳动力短缺和除草剂禁用加剧，机器人可能成为少数可行方案之一，其价值将进一步提升。文章提供了农场主评估机器人投资回报的新视角，对智慧畜牧和精准农业从业者具有重要参考价值。
featuredReason: 本文颠覆了“技术产品必然降价”的惯性思维，从经济价值角度重新定义农业机器人的定价逻辑，对智慧畜牧行业评估自动化设备投资回报具有直接指导意义。
contentFull: Opinion | Why agricultural robots may never become much cheaper 13-07 | Crop solutions | Expert opinionLinkedInFacebookXWhatsAppPrint Geert Hekkert Chief editor of Future FarmingThe Andela Robot Weeder carries a price tag of approximately €800,000 (US$961,000) in the Future Farming Buying Guide. But is that price driven by the cost of manufacturing the robot, or by the value it delivers on the farm? Photo: Jan Willem Schouten.Watch an autonomous weeding robot at a field demonstration today and most people expect one thing: in a few years these machines will be (much) cheaper. After all, that is what happened with GPS guidance, drones, cameras and computers. More manufacturers, larger production volumes and cheaper electronics usually lead to lower prices.But I'm no longer convinced the same will happen with agricultural robots. In fact, there are good reasons why autonomous weeders may not become significantly cheaper over time. During a recent field demonstration in the Netherlands, Joost Rijk of Wageningen University & Research offered an interesting perspective. His reasoning is surprisingly simple. Manufacturers cannot price a robot above what growers can economically justify. If they do, sales disappear. But pricing it much lower also makes little sense, because they would simply give away profit.Value on the farmIn other words, the selling price of a robot is not determined by what it costs to build. It is determined by the value it creates on the farm. That is a fundamentally different way of looking at machinery. Traditionally, growers compare machines mainly on purchase price. With robots, however, the discussion is shifting towards economic value. How much labour does the robot replace? How difficult has it become to find reliable workers? How much herbicide can it eliminate? Does the crop perform better without chemical weed control? Can one operator supervise several robots at the same time? And perhaps most importantly: will the machine reliably perform during the short periods when every hour counts?These questions make calculating a business case more complicated than ever. They also make it much harder to identify the "best buy". A robot that makes little financial sense on one farm may deliver an excellent return on another. Some manufacturers openly acknowledge this reality. During the same event, Bas Andela,founder of Dutch manufacturer Andela Techniek, summed it up perfectly: "Don't ask what the robot costs. Ask what it earns the grower."What are robots really competing against?Perhaps that is the more interesting question. Today, many growers still compare a weeding robot with a sprayer, a mechanical hoe or seasonal labour. But what happens if more herbicides disappear from the market? Or if labour shortages continue to worsen? The comparison changes completely. The robot no longer has to compete with chemical weed control on price. Instead, it may become one of the few practical solutions left. That changes the entire pricing discussion.Agricultural robots will undoubtedly become faster, smarter and more reliable over the next decade. But that does not automatically mean they will become cheaper. Just as a 300 hp tractor is not priced solely according to the amount of steel it contains, robots will increasingly be valued according to the economic benefits they deliver on the farm.There is, however, one important difference. Many farmers have a strong emotional connection with a particular tractor brand. Years of experience, family history, dealer relationships and brand loyalty all influence purchasing decisions. Just as in the automotive industry, customers are often willing to pay more for the brand they believe in. I'm not sure the same will ever happen with agricultural robots. Farmers may still fall in love with the colour of a tractor's bonnet. But when it comes to robots, I suspect they’ll fall in love with the business case instead.LinkedInFacebookXWhatsAppPrintMore aboutautonomous farmfarm managementmechanical weederroboticsweed controladLEARN MORERelated articles25-06 | NewsFieldwork Robotics secures new funding to accelerate raspberry harvesting robotPremium24-06 | ArticleAustralian vegetable farm puts 2 weeding technologies to the testPremium23-06 | ArticleTested: first Einböck intra-row precision hoePremium12-06 | Expert opinionMechanical weeding loses momentum Buyers guides & Catalogues Subscribe to our YouTube channel Taiwan Smart Agriweek 2026Sep 8-10, 2026 TaiNEX 1, Tapei
translationZh:
观看田间演示中的自主除草机器人时，大多数人都会期待一件事：几年后这些机器会（大幅）降价。毕竟，GPS导航、无人机、摄像头和计算机都曾经历过这样的过程。更多的制造商、更大的产量和更便宜的电子元件通常会导致价格下降。

但我已不再确信农业机器人也会如此。事实上，有充分理由表明自主除草机可能不会随时间推移而显著降价。在荷兰最近的一次田间演示中，瓦赫宁根大学与研究中心的Joost Rijk提供了一个有趣的视角。他的推理出奇地简单。制造商不能将机器人定价高于种植者经济上能承受的水平。如果定价过高，销量就会消失。但定价过低也毫无意义，因为他们等于白白放弃利润。

**农场上的价值**

换句话说，机器人的售价并非由其制造成本决定，而是由它在农场上创造的价值决定。这是一种看待机械的截然不同的方式。传统上，种植者主要根据购买价格来比较机器。然而，对于机器人，讨论正转向经济价值。机器人能替代多少劳动力？找到可靠工人变得有多困难？它能减少多少除草剂的使用？不使用化学除草，作物表现是否更好？一名操作员能否同时监管多台机器人？也许最重要的是：在每一小时都至关重要的短暂作业窗口期，机器能否可靠运行？

这些问题使得计算商业案例比以往任何时候都更加复杂。它们也让识别“最佳购买”变得困难得多。在一家农场经济上不划算的机器人，可能在另一家农场带来极好的回报。一些制造商公开承认这一现实。在同一场活动中，荷兰制造商Andela Techniek的创始人Bas Andela完美地总结道：“不要问机器人多少钱。要问它能为种植者赚多少钱。”

**机器人真正的竞争对手是什么？**

也许这才是更有趣的问题。如今，许多种植者仍将除草机器人比作喷雾器、机械中耕机或季节性劳动力。但如果更多除草剂从市场上消失呢？或者如果劳动力短缺持续恶化呢？比较将完全改变。机器人不再需要在价格上与化学除草竞争。相反，它可能成为少数几个可行的解决方案之一。这完全改变了定价逻辑。
author: Geert Hekkert
contentHtml: Opinion | Why agricultural robots may never become much cheaper Watch an autonomous weeding robot
